label: Paris — Île de la Cité
sub: Historic heart of France and seat of national power since the Capetian dynasty. Site of Notre-Dame Cathedral, the Palais de Justice, and the original Merovingian settlement on the Seine.

label: Palace of Versailles
sub: Seat of the French royal court from 1682 until the Revolution in 1789. Built by Louis XIV as the supreme symbol of French absolute monarchy; now a UNESCO World Heritage Site and museum.

label: Bastille (Demolished)
sub: Medieval fortress-prison stormed on July 14, 1789, marking the outbreak of the French Revolution. Demolished by 1792; the Place de la Bastille now occupies the site and hosts the Colonne de Juillet.

label: Verdun Battlefields
sub: Site of the longest and bloodiest battle of World War I (Feb–Dec 1916), claiming ~700,000 casualties. The Ossuary of Douaumont contains the remains of over 130,000 unidentified soldiers.

label: Normandy — D-Day Beaches
sub: Site of Operation Overlord (June 6, 1944), the largest amphibious assault in history. Allied forces landed at Utah, Omaha, Gold, Juno, and Sword beaches, beginning the liberation of Western Europe from Nazi occupation.

label: Reims Cathedral
sub: Gothic masterpiece where French kings were traditionally crowned from 816 CE (Louis the Pious) to 1825 (Charles X). Baptism of Clovis I nearby in 496 CE established the sacred link between the Frankish crown and Christianity.

label: Strasbourg — European Institutions
sub: Seat of the European Parliament and Council of Europe. Strasbourg and its region (Alsace) was the object of Franco-German rivalry for centuries, changing hands in 1871, 1919, 1940, and finally returned to France in 1944.

label: Vichy — WWII Collaborationist Capital
sub: Spa town that became the seat of the État français under Marshal Pétain (July 1940 – August 1944). The Vichy regime collaborated with Nazi Germany, including in the deportation of over 75,000 Jews to death camps.

label: Agincourt (Azincourt)
sub: Site of the Battle of Agincourt (October 25, 1415), where Henry V's English army devastated the French nobility. An estimated 6,000–10,000 French were killed vs. fewer than 400 English, cementing the longbow's dominance.

label: Orléans — Siege Lifted by Joan of Arc
sub: English forces besieged Orléans from October 1428. Joan of Arc arrived in April 1429 and lifted the siege within nine days (May 8), the turning point of the Hundred Years' War. The city celebrates her liberation annually.

label: Rouen — Joan of Arc Executed
sub: Joan of Arc was burned at the stake in the old market square of Rouen (Place du Vieux-Marché) on May 30, 1431, after being condemned for heresy by a pro-English ecclesiastical court. She was canonized in 1920.

label: Avignon — Papal Seat (1309–1376)
sub: The papacy relocated to Avignon (1309–1376) under pressure from Philip IV, during which time seven popes ruled from the Palais des Papes. The 'Babylonian Captivity' of the papacy gave French kings enormous influence over the Church.

label: Calais — Last English Continental Territory
sub: English forces captured Calais in 1347 and held it for 211 years until France retook it in 1558 during the reign of Mary I of England. Its loss prompted Mary's famous deathbed lament. Now the gateway for cross-channel transport.

label: Fontainebleau — Napoleon's Abdication
sub: Napoleon signed his first abdication at the Château de Fontainebleau on April 11, 1814 (the 'Fontainebleau Treaty'), exiling himself to Elba. He returned in March 1815 for the Hundred Days before his final defeat at Waterloo.

label: Marseille — France's Oldest City
sub: Founded by Greek colonists (600 BCE), France's largest Mediterranean port and second-largest city. Marseille was the gateway for troops from France's North African colonies in both World Wars and remains a focal point of immigration and multiculturalism.

label: Lyon — Resistance Capital
sub: France's second city during the Second Empire and historical capital of French silk industry. During WWII, Lyon was the headquarters of the French Resistance network, led by Jean Moulin under de Gaulle's orders until Moulin's arrest in 1943.

label: Élysée Palace — Presidential Residence
sub: Official residence and principal workplace of the President of the French Republic since 1873. Built in 1722 for the Comte d'Évreux, it has been the center of French executive power throughout the Fifth Republic.

label: Hall of Mirrors — 1871 & 1919 Treaties
sub: The Hall of Mirrors at Versailles was the site of two pivotal moments: proclamation of the German Empire (Jan 18, 1871) humiliating France, and the signing of the Treaty of Versailles (Jun 28, 1919) ending WWI and imposing war guilt on Germany.

label: Chartres Cathedral
sub: One of the finest examples of French High Gothic architecture, built 1194–1220. Its famous blue stained-glass windows survived WWII thanks to their removal for safekeeping. A UNESCO World Heritage Site and pilgrimage destination since medieval times.

label: Alsace-Lorraine — Contested Province
sub: Region seized by Germany after the Franco-Prussian War (1871), recovered by France in 1919, re-annexed by Nazi Germany (1940), and liberated in 1944. The region's French-German identity and Concordat exception (no separation of church and state) reflect its unique history.

label: Mont Saint-Michel
sub: Tidal island monastery off the Normandy coast, fortified since the 8th century. A pilgrimage site and symbol of French architectural ingenuity, it was fortified against English attack during the Hundred Years' War and never fell. UNESCO World Heritage Site.

label: Bordeaux — Wine Capital
sub: Major Atlantic port and wine-producing region, known since Roman times. During the Franco-Prussian War and both World Wars, the French government temporarily relocated to Bordeaux. Its historical center is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

label: Vélodrome d'Hiver — Holocaust Memorial
sub: Site where French police detained 13,152 Jews in July 1942 before deportation to Auschwitz. The building was demolished in 1959; a memorial now marks the spot near the Eiffel Tower. Chirac's 1995 speech acknowledged French state responsibility.

label: Bataclan — November 2015 Terrorist Attack
sub: Concert hall in Paris's 11th arrondissement where ISIS-directed gunmen killed 90 people on November 13, 2015. The attack, combined with simultaneous strikes at cafés and the Stade de France, resulted in 130 deaths and transformed French security policy.

label: Tours — Battle of Tours/Poitiers (732)
sub: Approximate site of the Battle of Tours (also called Poitiers), where Charles Martel's Frankish army defeated the Umayyad Muslim force in 732 CE, halting the northward advance of Islam into Western Europe.

label: Compiègne — Armistice Sites (1918 & 1940)
sub: The Forest of Compiègne is where the WWI Armistice was signed on November 11, 1918. Hitler humiliated France by forcing the 1940 armistice to be signed in the same railway carriage. The Armistice Clearing is now a national memorial.

label: Lascaux — Prehistoric Cave Paintings
sub: Cave complex in the Dordogne region containing some of the most remarkable Paleolithic cave paintings in the world, dating to approximately 17,000 years ago. Discovered in 1940, closed to the public in 1963; a replica (Lascaux II) receives visitors.
